annotations.text: The fact that therapy with carbamazepine had been recently initiated focuses the differential diagnosis on cutaneous reactions to drugs.

Toxic epidermal necrolysis is thought to be a cell-mediated immunologic reaction to drug metabolites, which may accumulate because of abnormal metabolism of the drug, resulting in the persistence of immunogenic metabolites.

The most important consideration is the staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, which is mediated by the exotoxin (phage group II, type 71) produced by  *S. aureus* ; it produces diffuse ill-defined erythema and flaccid bullae, with a positive Nikolsky's sign
The extent of involvement and the clinical history in this patient are virtually diagnostic of this disorder.
The necrosis appears to be mediated by CD8+ T cells by way of Fas-mediated apoptosis.
This condition affects most children with this illness to a variable degree and is often quite severe.

The differential diagnosis of the alveolar opacities includes pulmonary hemorrhage, pneumonia, or pulmonary edema; in this clinical setting, the progressive changes seen on the films best fit the pattern of the acute respiratory distress syndrome.
Most deaths in patients with toxic epidermal necrolysis are a result of sepsis-induced organ failure.

Corticosteroid drugs have also been advocated for toxic epidermal necrolysis, but most clinicians do not support their routine use, given the risk of increased infection and the absence of data demonstrating benefit.
Published mortality rates for patients with toxic epidermal necrolysis vary widely and are between 40 and 70 percent.
